content.text: Concurrency in asyncio doesn't mean unlimited parallelism — uncontrolled concurrency can exhaust file descriptors, overwhelm APIs with rate limits, or crash databases with too many simultaneous connections. A

is asyncio's built-in mechanism for bounding how many coroutines can actively execute a shared resource operation at the same time, acting as a numeric gatekeeper that enforces a hard cap on concurrent access.
is a synchronization primitive that maintains an internal counter initialized to a given value

. Each time a coroutine acquires the semaphore, the counter decrements by one; each time it releases, the counter increments by one. When the counter reaches zero, any further acquisition attempts block — the coroutine suspends and waits in a queue until another coroutine releases, incrementing the counter back above zero.
at any moment. If you create
, at most 5 coroutines can be inside the guarded section simultaneously; all others wait.
if the counter is ever incremented beyond its initial value — protecting against release-without-acquire bugs.
Async code naturally fans out into thousands of concurrent coroutines, especially when processing lists of URLs, records, or tasks. Without a cap:
receive thousands of simultaneous requests, triggering rate-limit errors (429) or IP bans.
are exhausted, causing connection errors under load.
run out, crashing the process with "too many open files."
back-pressure or block the caller entirely.
gives a clean, idiomatic way to express "do this work concurrently, but never more than N operations at a time," without complex manual tracking of active task counts.
Think of a semaphore as a parking lot with

spaces. Every coroutine is a car. To enter the lot (perform the guarded work), a car needs a parking space. If all spaces are taken, the car waits at the entrance until one opens up. When a car leaves (releases), its space becomes available and the next waiting car enters. The total number of cars parked at any one moment never exceeds
— that's the invariant the semaphore guarantees.
, append it to the waiting deque, and
If there are waiters, resolve the oldest
— the first waiting coroutine is scheduled to resume (FIFO order).

Because asyncio runs on a single-threaded event loop, there are no race conditions between checking the value and modifying it — the check and the suspend/increment are atomic from the event loop's perspective.
The following diagram shows the lifecycle of coroutines competing for a semaphore with a limit of 2.---
The idiomatic way to use a semaphore is as an async context manager. It automatically acquires on entry and releases on exit, even if the body raises an exception.

import asyncio

sem = asyncio.Semaphore(3)  # at most 3 concurrent holders

async def fetch(session, url):
    async with sem:            # blocks here if 3 others are inside
        # do the actual I/O work
        await asyncio.sleep(0)  # simulate async operation
        return url

async def main():
    urls = [f"https://example.com/{i}" for i in range(10)]
    tasks = [fetch(None, url) for url in urls]
    results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
    print(results)

asyncio.run(main())

await sem.acquire()
try:
    # guarded work
finally:
    sem.release()
pairs are fragile if the body raises an exception before
is called, which permanently depletes the semaphore.
A common production pattern: scrape 500 URLs concurrently but limit to 10 simultaneous open connections to respect server rate limits.

import asyncio
import aiohttp

CONCURRENCY_LIMIT = 10

async def fetch_url(session: aiohttp.ClientSession, sem: asyncio.Semaphore, url: str) -> dict:
    async with sem:
        try:
            async with session.get(url, timeout=aiohttp.ClientTimeout(total=10)) as resp:
                body = await resp.text()
                return {"url": url, "status": resp.status, "length": len(body)}
        except aiohttp.ClientError as e:
            return {"url": url, "error": str(e)}

async def scrape_all(urls: list[str]) -> list[dict]:
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(CONCURRENCY_LIMIT)
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        tasks = [fetch_url(session, sem, url) for url in urls]
        return await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

async def main():
    urls = [f"https://httpbin.org/get?n={i}" for i in range(50)]
    results = await scrape_all(urls)
    print(f"Completed {len(results)} requests")

asyncio.run(main())
Here every one of the 50 tasks is created immediately and starts running, but the
line gates execution — at any instant, at most 10 coroutines are inside the session.get block. The rest are suspended at the

import asyncio

async def main():
    sem = asyncio.BoundedSemaphore(2)
    await sem.acquire()
    sem.release()
    sem.release()  # back to initial value, fine
    sem.release()  # raises ValueError: BoundedSemaphore released too many times
    
asyncio.run(main())
during development to catch asymmetric acquire/release pairs early. Switch to plain
only if you intentionally need releases that can exceed the initial value (uncommon).
Scoping — where to create the semaphore
The semaphore must be shared among all coroutines you want to limit together. Common patterns:

# Pattern 1: module-level or class-level (shared across all calls)
_db_sem = asyncio.Semaphore(5)

async def db_query(sql):
    async with _db_sem:
        ...

# Pattern 2: per-batch (scoped to one run)
async def process_batch(items):
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(10)
    tasks = [process(sem, item) for item in items]
    await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

async def process(sem, item):
    async with sem:
        ...
A semaphore created inside an individual coroutine is useless — each invocation gets its own instance with its own independent counter, so there is no shared limit between calls.

# With gather (Python 3.7+)
async def main():
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(5)
    tasks = [do_work(sem, i) for i in range(100)]
    await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

# With TaskGroup (Python 3.11+, preferred — propagates exceptions cleanly)
async def main():
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(5)
    async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
        for i in range(100):
            tg.create_task(do_work(sem, i))
If a coroutine should not wait indefinitely for the semaphore, wrap the acquisition with

async def do_work_with_timeout(sem: asyncio.Semaphore, item):
    try:
        async with asyncio.timeout(5.0):  # Python 3.11+
            async with sem:
                await perform_operation(item)
    except TimeoutError:
        print(f"Could not acquire semaphore in 5s for {item}")
Calling an external API that enforces rate limits (e.g., 10 concurrent requests max).
Using a database connection pool where you own the pool sizing.
Performing parallel file I/O where too many open file handles cause OS errors.
Running CPU-bound tasks in a thread pool via
Batched inference against an LLM API to stay within TPM (tokens per minute) limits.
You need mutual exclusion (exactly 1 holder at a time) — use
, but semantically clearer and enforces the same-task-release rule).
You need to signal between producers and consumers that work is ready — use
You need readers/writer separation — compose a lock with counters manually or use a library like
You need cross-process limits — asyncio semaphores are in-process only; use Redis, a database advisory lock, or a token bucket service for distributed rate limiting.
1. Creating the semaphore inside the coroutine being limited

# WRONG — each task gets its own semaphore; no shared limit
async def fetch(url):
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(5)   # new semaphore each call
    async with sem:
        ...

# RIGHT — one semaphore passed to all tasks
sem = asyncio.Semaphore(5)
async def fetch(url):
    async with sem:
        ...
2. Using manual acquire/release without a try/finally

# WRONG — if do_io() raises, release() is never called; semaphore leaks
async def bad(sem):
    await sem.acquire()
    await do_io()       # exception here = semaphore permanently depleted
    sem.release()

# RIGHT
async def good(sem):
    async with sem:     # release is guaranteed even on exception
        await do_io()
3. Forgetting that a Semaphore(1) is not the same as a Lock semantically
can be released by a different coroutine than the one that acquired it. An
enforces ownership — only the acquirer can release it. If you need ownership semantics, use
4. Choosing a limit that is too high or too low

Too high: you still overwhelm the downstream service. Too low: throughput collapses because tasks spend most of their time waiting for the semaphore rather than doing work. The right value comes from profiling and knowledge of the downstream system's capacity (connection pool size, rate limit headers, etc.).
5. Not sharing the semaphore across retry loops

If a task retries on failure, each retry attempt should still be guarded by the same semaphore. A common mistake is catching an exception and immediately re-calling the function without re-entering the semaphore guard, creating a burst of uncontrolled retries.
Pass the semaphore as an argument
to the guarded coroutine rather than using a global. This makes the dependency explicit, aids testing with mock semaphores, and avoids coupling to global state.
in comments or by variable name (e.g.,
to catch bugs early; swap to
only when you deliberately need extra releases.
in high-throughput systems — a semaphore that is frequently at zero is a bottleneck signal and the limit may need raising, or the guarded operation may need optimization.
— do not use one semaphore to limit both database and HTTP connections; they have different capacities and mixing them causes unnecessary coupling.
for retries: when a task fails and retries, it should re-acquire the semaphore and add a jitter delay so retrying tasks don't synchronize and create a thundering herd.
itself adds negligible overhead — it is a pure-Python object with a counter and a deque. The real performance effect is the throttling behavior it enforces.
with guarded operations averaging 200 ms yields a maximum throughput of
operations per second. Raising the limit linearly raises throughput until the downstream system saturates.
— if downstream slows, guarded operations take longer, the semaphore stays depleted longer, and upstream tasks queue up. This is the correct behavior; the semaphore acts as a flow-control valve.

is generally not a concern: asyncio semaphores use a FIFO waiting queue, so every blocked coroutine is guaranteed to eventually acquire the semaphore in the order it arrived. There is no priority inheritance.
scales with the number of waiting coroutines (one
per waiter), which is bounded by the number of tasks created minus the semaphore limit. For typical batch sizes (thousands), this is negligible.
Mutual exclusion of a critical section
Same as Semaphore, but raises on over-release
that additionally checks that only the holding task can release it. If you need mutual exclusion and the same coroutine always acquires and releases in the same call frame,
is the clearer and safer choice.
is preferable whenever multiple units of a resource are available or when producer/consumer patterns pass the "token" between coroutines.

is low with asyncio (there is no preemption and no true blocking), but logical deadlocks can occur if two coroutines each hold a different semaphore and wait for the other — the classic circular-wait condition. Avoid acquiring multiple semaphores simultaneously, or always acquire them in the same fixed order.
is the most practical reliability concern. Always use

so that exceptions inside the guarded block automatically release the semaphore. Leaked acquisitions deplete the counter permanently and starve all future tasks, making this a hard-to-detect, latent failure.
is cancelled, asyncio properly removes it from the internal waiter deque and the counter is not decremented. This is correct behavior and prevents ghost acquisitions.
: asyncio semaphores live in one process's memory. For distributed systems requiring cross-process limits (e.g., across Kubernetes pods), use Redis
/ Lua scripts, a distributed token bucket, or an API gateway rate-limiter at the infrastructure level.
: asyncio semaphores are not thread-safe. If mixing asyncio with threads (
), access the semaphore only from async coroutines on the event loop thread — never call
directly from a thread pool worker.
limits concurrent access to a shared resource to exactly
coroutines at a time; all others suspend and queue in FIFO order.
— it guarantees release on both normal completion and exceptions.
The semaphore must be shared among all competing coroutines; creating it inside the guarded function gives each call its own independent counter, defeating the limit.
Choose the limit based on the downstream resource's capacity — too high overwhelms the service, too low bottlenecks throughput unnecessarily.
during development to detect asymmetric acquire/release bugs.
Semaphore is not a substitute for
when ownership matters — only the exact coroutine that acquired a
Asyncio semaphores are in-process only; distributed rate limiting requires an external coordination layer like Redis or an API gateway.
